---
title: "Food Deserts and Health Outcomes: A Census Tract-Level Analysis"
format:
  pdf:
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.15
  html:
    self-contained: true
    theme: cosmo
---

**Group Members:** Jiaoer Ma, Jian Shi | **Section:** Tue/Thu 11:00am | **GitHub:** JiaoerM

---

## Research Question

To what extent do food deserts correlate with chronic disease rates at the U.S. census
tract level, and does this relationship hold after controlling for income, race, and
geography? If food desert status independently predicts worse health outcomes beyond
what poverty alone explains, that strengthens the case for targeted food access
interventions over purely income-support programs.

## Data & Approach

We merged two datasets on the 11-digit census tract FIPS code, yielding **56,327 tracts
across 48 states**. The **USDA Food Access Research Atlas (2019)** classifies tracts as
food deserts if they are both low-income and low-access (>1 mile from a supermarket in
urban areas, >10 miles in rural). The **CDC PLACES (2022)** dataset provides model-based
prevalence estimates for diabetes, obesity, high blood pressure, and smoking at the tract
level. After cleaning, we computed group comparisons, Pearson correlations, OLS
regressions with controls, and income-stratified breakdowns. We then built a five-tab
Streamlit dashboard for interactive exploration.

The main technical difficulty was the FIPS merge: USDA stored tract IDs as floats
(e.g., `1001020100.0`) while CDC stored them as strings, causing a near-empty join until
both were cast to zero-padded 11-character strings. On deployment, Streamlit Cloud
required converting all local file paths to relative paths and fixing a Plotly choropleth
bug where full state names needed mapping to 2-letter abbreviations.

## Static Plots

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
import pandas as pd, numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

df = pd.read_csv("data/derived/merged_tract_data.csv", dtype={"CensusTract": str})
OUTCOMES = ["DIABETES", "OBESITY", "BPHIGH", "CSMOKING"]
FLAG = "LILATracts_1And10"
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Fig 1 (left): Food desert tracts have higher prevalence across all four health outcomes. Fig 2 (right): Correlation matrix — income is the strongest predictor (r = −0.60 for diabetes), but the food desert flag shows independent positive correlations (r = 0.15–0.22)."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 3.8

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.8))

# Bar chart
ax = axes[0]
group = df.groupby(FLAG)[OUTCOMES].mean()
group.index = ["Non-Desert", "Food Desert"]
x, w = np.arange(len(OUTCOMES)), 0.35
ax.bar(x - w/2, group.loc["Non-Desert"],  w, label="Non-Desert",  color="#4C72B0", alpha=0.85)
ax.bar(x + w/2, group.loc["Food Desert"], w, label="Food Desert", color="#DD8452", alpha=0.85)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(["Diabetes", "Obesity", "High BP", "Smoking"], fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("Prevalence (%)", fontsize=9)
ax.set_title("Fig 1. Health Outcomes by Food Desert Status", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

# Correlation heatmap
ax2 = axes[1]
cols = OUTCOMES + [FLAG, "MedianFamilyIncome", "TractHUNV", "TractSNAP"]
labels = {"DIABETES":"Diabetes","OBESITY":"Obesity","BPHIGH":"High BP",
          "CSMOKING":"Smoking","LILATracts_1And10":"Food\nDesert",
          "MedianFamilyIncome":"Income","TractHUNV":"No Vehicle","TractSNAP":"SNAP"}
cm = df[cols].dropna().corr()
cm.index = [labels[c] for c in cm.index]
cm.columns = [labels[c] for c in cm.columns]
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(cm, dtype=bool))
sns.heatmap(cm, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="RdBu_r",
            center=0, vmin=-1, vmax=1, square=True, linewidths=0.4,
            annot_kws={"size": 7}, ax=ax2, cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.7})
ax2.set_title("Fig 2. Correlation Matrix", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")
ax2.tick_params(labelsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Fig 3 (left): Diabetes rates by income quartile split by food desert status. The gap persists even in Q4 (highest income), suggesting food access has an effect independent of income. Fig 4 (right): Scatter of population >1 mile from grocery vs. diabetes (5,000 sampled tracts). Orange = food desert, blue = non-desert."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 3.5

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.5))

# Income quartile bar
ax = axes[0]
df["income_q"] = pd.qcut(df["MedianFamilyIncome"], q=4,
                          labels=["Q1\n(Low)","Q2","Q3","Q4\n(High)"])
qm = df.dropna(subset=["income_q"]).groupby(["income_q", FLAG])["DIABETES"].mean().reset_index()
qs = ["Q1\n(Low)","Q2","Q3","Q4\n(High)"]
x, w = np.arange(len(qs)), 0.35
for flag, offset, color, label in [(0,-w/2,"#4C72B0","Non-Desert"),(1,w/2,"#DD8452","Food Desert")]:
    vals = [qm[(qm["income_q"]==q)&(qm[FLAG]==flag)]["DIABETES"].values for q in qs]
    vals = [v[0] if len(v) else np.nan for v in vals]
    ax.bar(x + offset, vals, w, label=label, color=color, alpha=0.85)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(qs, fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("Diabetes (%)", fontsize=9)
ax.set_title("Fig 3. Diabetes by Income Quartile", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

# Scatter
ax2 = axes[1]
s = df.dropna(subset=["lapop1","DIABETES"]).sample(5000, random_state=42)
colors = s[FLAG].map({0:"#4C72B0", 1:"#DD8452"})
ax2.scatter(s["lapop1"], s["DIABETES"], c=colors, alpha=0.3, s=5, rasterized=True)
for flag, color in [(0,"#1a4f99"),(1,"#a94c0a")]:
    sub = s[s[FLAG]==flag][["lapop1","DIABETES"]].dropna()
    m, b = np.polyfit(sub["lapop1"], sub["DIABETES"], 1)
    xl = np.linspace(sub["lapop1"].min(), sub["lapop1"].max(), 200)
    ax2.plot(xl, m*xl+b, color=color, linewidth=2)
r, _ = stats.pearsonr(s["lapop1"], s["DIABETES"])
ax2.set_xlabel("Pop. >1mi from Grocery", fontsize=9)
ax2.set_ylabel("Diabetes (%)", fontsize=9)
ax2.set_title(f"Fig 4. Access vs. Diabetes (r = {r:.3f})", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold")
ax2.grid(alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Fig 1 shows food desert tracts have consistently higher disease rates (diabetes +3.1pp,
obesity +6.6pp, high BP +4.9pp, smoking +4.6pp; all p < 0.001). Fig 2 shows income is
the strongest single predictor (r = −0.60 for diabetes), but the food desert flag carries
independent signal. Fig 3 is the key confounding test: the gap persists within every
income quartile including the highest, suggesting food access matters beyond poverty.
Fig 4 confirms the positive relationship between grocery distance and diabetes rates.

## Streamlit App

The dashboard has five tabs filtered by a global sidebar (health outcome, state,
urban/rural, income range). The **Correlation Explorer** plots any food access measure
against any health outcome with live Pearson r and regression lines by food desert group.
The **State Map** shows a choropleth switchable between food desert prevalence and each
health outcome. **Group Comparison** displays bar charts, violin plots, and the
income-quartile breakdown. **Regression** runs a live OLS model with a coefficient table
and 95% CI plot. **Tract Explorer** provides a searchable table with a live correlation
heatmap. Together the tabs allow users to interrogate the food desert-health relationship
geographically, statistically, and with flexible confounder controls.

## Limitations

This analysis is cross-sectional and cannot establish causality. There is a temporal
mismatch between the USDA (2019) and CDC (2022) data. All results are at the tract level
and subject to the ecological fallacy. The binary food desert definition obscures
continuous variation in access, and unmeasured confounders (healthcare access, built
environment) likely explain part of the observed associations.
